3	**Predaja: Petak 3.4.2026.**
4	
5	## Zadaci i bodovanje
6	
7	| Kriterij | Bodovi |
8	| --- | --- |
9	| Granularno izvođenje agenta po taskovima | 1 |
10	| Ispitivanje agenta za pojašnjenja | 1 |
11	| Usmeno ispitivanje LINQ/await ili C# koncepata | 2 |

13	- [ ]  Dodati javni GitHub repozitorij koji će biti za rad na projektu (sve slati na ‘main’ branch)
14	    - [ ]  Javiti na mail GitHub repo link **najkasnije do 20.3.**
15	    - [ ]  Sav kod koji se ocjenjuje mora biti na GH repozitoriju
16	    - [ ]  U root folder repozitorija dodati folder lab-1 gdje je potrebno uploadati “log” koristenja AI agenta
17	- [ ]  Potrebno je kreirati objektni model prema vlastitom projektnom zadatku, koji se sastoji od barem 7 klasa, gdje 4 klase moraju biti kompleksne (preko 5 svojstava). Svojstva trebaju biti smislena i dobrog tipa:
18	- barem jedan vlastiti enum
19	- barem jedno DateTime svojstvo
20	- ispravne konekcije 1-N, N-N
21	- [ ]  Potrebno je u main programu “napuniti” objekte podacima - kreirati razgranato barem 3 glavna objekta.
22	- primjeice, ako je tema aplikacije “Nogometni turnir”, potrebno je imati barem 3 turnira s po barem 3 ekipe.
23	- [ ]  U main programu je potrebno definirati smislene LINQ upite nad objektnim modelom koji će se kasnije (možda) koristiti u samoj aplikaciji
24	    - [ ]  Potrebno je razumjeti LINQ naredbe koje su dodane i moći ih modificirati uz pomoć AI agenta
25	- [ ]  Proučiti i razumjeti koncept async-await
26	- [ ]  Sav kod treba biti na GH **najkasnije do 2.4.2026. u 18:00**
27	
28	# Osnove C# jezika
29	
30	U ovom će se poglavlju prezentirati osnovni pojmovi i koncepti koji će biti korišteni na kolegiju.
31	
32	## Solution i projekti
33	
34	U pravilu se svaka ozbiljnija aplikacija dijeli na nekoliko slojeva. Ti slojevi su najčešće prezentacijski sloj, model/business/services sloj i sloj za pristup bazi podataka (Data Access Layer ili kraće DAL sloj). Kod razvoja višeslojnih aplikacija, potrebno je uvesti i nezavisnosti između slojeva u „smjeru prema gore", što znači da prezentacijski sloj može biti ovisan o DAL ili model sloju, ali DAL ili model sloj ne smiju biti ovisni o prezentacijskom sloju. U .NET okruženju se takva raspodjela najelegantnije postiže raspodjelom slojeva na projekte unutar rješenja (solutiona). Po uzoru na tu ideju, napravit ćemo novi projekt i nazvati ga Vjezba.Model. Novi projekt se dodaje odabirom opcije **File → Add → New Project** u izborniku.
35	
36	Ako pogledamo solution explorer, novi projekt je kreiran i automatski mu je dodana nova klasa Class1, koju možemo slobodno obrisati.
37	
38	## Klase i objekti, svojstva u klasi
39	
40	Nova klasa u projekt se dodaje tako da u solution explorer prozoru, na željenom projektu, odaberemo desni klik i add new item, te pronađemo odgovarajuću stavku iz izbornika.
41	
42	<aside>
43	⚠️
44	
45	**Važna napomena**: pri dodavanju nove klase njena vidljivost je automatski postavljena na „internal", te da bi klasa bila vidljiva u drugim projektima potrebno ju je definirati kao „public".
46	
47	</aside>
48	
49	Primjerice, ukoliko dodamo klasu **Fakultet** u projekt Vjezba.Model, moramo dodati ključnu riječ „public":
50	
51	```csharp
52	namespace Vjezba.Model
53	{
54	    public class Fakultet
55	    {
56	    }
57	}
58	```
59	
60	### Odnos klasa
61	
62	Klase mogu implementirati nasljeđivanje, što znači da izvedena klasa nasljeđuje svojstva i funkcije iz bazne klase. Klase također mogu biti u odnosu u kojem neka klasa sadrži niz referenci na objekte druge klase, najčešće u obliku liste. U nastavku je dan primjer, gdje klasa A nasljeđuje klasu B (B je bazna klasa), dok klasa A također ima u sebi listu referenci na objekte klase C (tzv. odnos 1-N).
63	
64	```csharp
65	public class B
66	{
67	}
68	
69	public class A : B
70	{
71	    private List<C> _objektiKlaseC;
72	}
73	
74	public class C
75	{
76	}
77	```
78	
79	### Svojstva i polja, konstruktori i metode
80	
81	Kada govorimo o informacijama koje su sadržane u klasama, najčešće koristimo termine kao što su svojstva (**properties**) i rijeđe polja (**fields**). Polje (član) je doslovno varijabla koja je sadržana unutar neke klase, i kad se kreira instanca neke klase, moguće je koristiti to polje kao i svaku drugu varijablu. Iz gornjeg isječka koda, član `_objektiKlaseC` je privatna varijabla, koja je tipa `List<C>`. U pravilu, članovi bi uvijek trebali biti privatni, što znači da se tim članovima ne može pristupiti nigdje izvan klase u kojoj se nalaze. Svojstvo, s druge strane, možemo promatrati kao getter i setter funkciju, samo napisanu malo drukčijom sintaksom nego što je to u C++ ili Java jeziku:
82	
83	```csharp
84	public class Osoba
85	{
86	    private string _adresa;
87	    public string Adresa
88	    {
89	        get
90	        {
91	            return this._adresa;
92	        }
93	        set
94	        {
95	            this._adresa = value;
96	        }
97	    }
98	}
99	```
100	
101	Ekvivalentno u Java/C++ stilu:
102	
103	```csharp
104	public class Osoba
105	{
106	    private string _adresa;
107	
108	    public string GetAdresa()
109	    {
110	        return this._adresa;
111	    }
112	
113	    public void SetAdresa(string value)
114	    {
115	        this._adresa = value;
116	    }
117	}
118	```
119	
120	U gornjoj klasi, svojstvo je **Adresa**, a funkcije koje dohvaćaju ili postavljaju vrijednost tog svojstva su definirane ključnim riječima **get** i **set**. Vrijednost svojstva se u pravilu čuva u privatnom članu. Što se tiče prava pristupa, vrijede ista pravila kao i za polja – međutim kod svojstava se najčešće koristi pravo pristupa **public** ili **protected**. Postoje i automatska svojstva:
121	
122	```csharp
123	public int SvojstvoX { get; set; }
124	```
125	
126	Zašto koristiti automatska svojstva a ne polja? Postoje razlike kako C# doživljava varijable i svojstva. Držat ćemo se sljedećih pravila:
127	
128	- Klasa ne smije sadržavati javna polja
129	- Klasa bi trebala sadržavati svojstva (javna ili protected, u rijetkim slučajevima private)
130	- Koristiti automatsko svojstvo gdje je moguće
131	
132	Pri kreiranju objekta, prva funkcija koja se poziva unutar klase je **konstruktor**. Da bi klasa funkcionirala, nije potrebno napraviti konstruktor – osnovni (default) konstruktor će biti dodan automatski. Međutim, u nekim slučajevima je dobro dodati konstruktor za osiguravanje inicijalizacije:
133	
134	```csharp
135	public class A : B
136	{
137	    public List<C> ObjektiKlaseC { get; set; }
138	
139	    public A()
140	    {
141	        ObjektiKlaseC = new List<C>();
142	    }
143	}
144	```
145	
146	U gornjem slučaju je dobro koristiti konstruktor jer tada možemo inicijalizirati na ispravan način kompleksni objekt – kreirati mu novu instancu. Kada konstruktora ne bi bilo, onaj tko koristi tu klasu mora samostalno inicijalizirati listu, te ukoliko ju pokuša koristiti bez inicijalizacije, može dobiti **null reference** iznimku.
147	
148	Uz konstruktore i svojstva, gotovo svaka klasa sadrži niz **metoda** – funkcija koje predstavljaju implementaciju ponašanja neke klase. Funkcija može imati razine pristupa **private**, **protected** i **public**, a može biti zavisna od postojanja objekta ili nezavisna (**static**).
149	
150	```csharp
151	public int PrebrojiKolikoJeUListi()
152	{
153	    int rezultat = 0;
154	    foreach (var x in ObjektiKlaseC)
155	        rezultat++;
156	    return rezultat;
157	}
158	```
159	
160	Funkcija je definirana:
161	
162	- Tipom povratne vrijednosti
163	- Imenom (predlaže se CamelCase)
164	- Parametrima koje prima (tip parametra i koliko ih ima)
165	
166	## Iznimke
167	
168	Kao i u drugim programskim jezicima, C# ima razvijen mehanizam rukovanja iznimkama. Iznimka se može dogoditi uslijed nepredviđenih ulaznih parametara, pogreške u kodu i sl. Iznimke se mogu baciti i uloviti, ali svaka iznimka koju možemo baciti ili uloviti nasljeđuje iz bazne klase `Exception`. U samom .NET radnom okviru ima niz već postojećih iznimki koje se mogu iskoristiti u nizu situacija, ali moguće je i kreirati vlastitu iznimku – to je ni manje ni više nego nova klasa koja nasljeđuje iz klase Exception ili iz bilo koje njoj izvedene klase. Za bacanje iznimki koristimo ključnu riječ **throw**, dok za hvatanje iznimki koristimo kombinaciju **try-catch-(finally)**.
169	
170	## Sučelja (interface)
171	
172	Sučelje je skup pravila kojim definiramo kako izgleda ili koja ponašanja sadrži neka klasa koja ga implementira. Primjerice, klasa koja implementira sučelje `IDisposable` sigurno sadrži metodu `Dispose`. Ovo sučelje također indicira da klasa koristi resurse koje bi trebalo osloboditi nakon što smo završili s korištenjem te klase. Prešutni dogovor u C# je da sučelja počinju slovom „I" (od Interface), te ih je na taj način lako uočiti i razlikovati od „običnih" klasa.
173	
174	## Kolekcije (generics)
175	
176	Gotovo svaka poslovna aplikacija će morati voditi evidenciju (pamtiti) informacije o nizu različitih podataka. U tu svrhu koriste se razne kolekcije koje nam omogućavaju niz funkcionalnosti za rukovanje ovisno o konkretnoj potrebi i kontekstu. U C# najčešće se koriste strogo tipizirane kolekcije (generics) – to su kolekcije koje mogu pamtiti samo određene tipove podataka, tj. objekte neke specifične klase.
177	
178	- **List** – kolekcija u koju se može spremiti bilo kakav objekt. Ovo je primjer netipizirane kolekcije. Elementima liste se može pristupati preko uglatih zagrada i indexa: `mojaLista[5]` → vraća 6. element liste. Povratna vrijednost je tipa `object`.
179	- **List<int>** – lista cjelobrojnih vrijednosti, ne možemo dodati ništa osim cjelobrojne (int) vrijednosti. Primjer: `int x = mojaIntLista[2];`
180	- **List<ContactData>** – lista objekata tipa ContactData
181	- **Dictionary<int, string>** – kolekcija u kojoj su vrijednosti spremljene pod nekim ključem. U ovoj kolekciji ključ je cjelobrojna vrijednost, a vrijednost je string. Pristupamo na način: `mojDict[2] = "Dva"`
182	
183	Najčešće korištena klasa je upravo `List<XX>`, gdje je XX odgovarajući tip.
184	
185	### Petlje i enumeriranje kolekcije
186	
187	Da bi pregledali elemente neke kolekcije, moramo koristiti neku vrstu petlje. U C# najčešće se koristi **foreach** petlja koja prolazi po svim elementima neke kolekcije. Primjer takve petlje nalazi se u implementaciji metode `PrebrojiKolikoJeUListi` iznad.
188	
189	---
190	
191	# C# - napredni koncepti
192	
193	U narednom poglavlju bit će obrađeni napredni koncepti u C# jeziku – konkretnije lambda izrazi i LINQ mehanizam. Stabla izraza (expression trees) su posebne strukture u C# jeziku koje omogućavaju definiranje koda koji će tek kasnije biti izvršen. LINQ koristi stabla izraza za manipulaciju kolekcijama, gdje se pomoću stabla izraza definira upit koji će tek naknadno biti izveden. U ovom poglavlju bit će obrađene osnove LINQ izraza za manipulacije kolekcijama te `Func<>`, `Predicate<>` i `Action<>` objekti kojima se definira funkcija u obliku varijable.
194	
195	## Lambda izrazi i LINQ
196	
197	Lambda izrazi se u osnovi mogu poistovjetiti s anonimnim ili inline funkcijama. Ideja manipulacije kolekcijama kroz LINQ je da svaka kolekcija, pozivom neke LINQ naredbe se transformira u novu kolekciju.
198	
199	### Lambda izrazi
200	
201	Pomoću lambda izraza se definira anonimna funkcija ili stablo izraza koje se koristi pri izvršavanju primjerice LINQ upita. Sintaksa lambda izraza je sljedeća:
202	
203	```csharp
204	p => p.Id < 3
205	```
206	
207	U gornjem izrazu, prvi parametar `p` (s lijeve strane operatora `=>`) označava jedan ulazni objekt kolekcije. S desne strane operatora `=>` se nalazi izraz koji koristi objekt `p`. Povratna vrijednost izraza s desne strane je `bool`. Gornji izraz je ekvivalentan ovom:
208	
209	```csharp
210	p =>
211	{
212	    if (p.Id < 3)
213	        return true;
214	    return false;
215	}
216	```
217	
218	### LINQ – naredba where
219	
220	Vjerojatno najčešće korištena metoda prilikom manipulacija kolekcijama – služi za filtriranje i obradu samo određenih podataka.
221	
222	```csharp
223	var quizesWithLowId = listaKvizova
224	    .Where(p => p.Id < 3);
225	```
226	
227	**Lambda izraz** – kao rezultat se vraćaju samo oni elementi iz kolekcije za koje je zadovoljen izraz, tj. za koje funkcija vraća `true`. Naredba `Where` se poziva nad kolekcijom objekata (primjerice `List<Quiz>`).
228	
229	### LINQ – naredba ToList
230	
231	Svaku kolekciju možemo eksplicitno pretvoriti u listu pozivom `ToList()` metode koja će stvoriti novi `List<>` objekt i sve elemente kolekcije prebaciti u listu.
232	
233	### LINQ – naredbe First, Single, FirstOrDefault i SingleOrDefault
234	
235	Gore navedene naredbe služe za izdvajanje samo jednog elementa iz kolekcije. Razlike:
236	
237	- **First** – ukoliko nema niti jednog elementa u kolekciji, poziv naredbe baca iznimku
238	- **FirstOrDefault** – ukoliko nema niti jednog elementa, vraća default vrijednost (za klase `null`, za `int` vrijednost `0`)
239	- **Single** – ukoliko ima više od jednog elementa ili je kolekcija prazna, baca iznimku
240	- **SingleOrDefault** – slično kao Single, no vraća default vrijednost umjesto iznimke
241	
242	### LINQ – naredba OrderBy i OrderByDescending
243	
244	Koristi se ukoliko je potrebno poredati kolekciju. `OrderByDescending` primjenjuje poredak u obrnutom redoslijedu od `OrderBy` poziva. Sve LINQ naredbe se mogu zajedno kombinirati.
245	
246	### LINQ – naredba Count
247	
248	Kao rezultat vraća broj elemenata u kolekciji. Može se kombinirati s ostalim LINQ upitima i naredbama.
249	
250	### LINQ – podupiti
251	
252	Čest je slučaj da je potrebno izdvojiti elemente kolekcije na temelju kolekcija koje sadrže. Unutar bilo kojeg LINQ upita, može se postaviti dodatni upit nad bilo kojom kolekcijom definiranom nekim svojstvom.
253	
254	## Func, Predicate i Action objekti
255	
256	Slično kao što su pokazivači na funkcije u C/C++, u C# je uveden novi set objekata koji omogućavaju spremanje funkcije u varijablu, te kasnije pozivanje te funkcije s željenim parametrima i dohvaćanje rezultata. Primjerice, funkcija `GetRandomOperation()` vraća objekt `Func<int,int,int>`. Taj objekt predstavlja funkciju koja ima dva parametra tipa `int` i povratnu vrijednost tipa `int`. Zadnji parametar predloška je uvijek tip povratne vrijednosti funkcije.
257	
258	**Primjer – Program.cs:**
259	
260	```csharp
261	namespace LINQHelperApp.Console
262	{
263	    class Program
264	    {
265	        static void Main(string[] args)
266	        {
267	            var calc = new Calculator();
268	            Func<int,int,int> fOp = calc.GetRandomOperation();
269	            int x = 10, y = 5;
270	            int rez = fOp(x, y);
271	            System.Console.WriteLine(rez);
272	            System.Console.ReadKey();
273	        }
274	    }
275	}
276	```
277	
278	Varijabla `fOp` je objekt koji tretiramo kao funkciju – pozivamo ga kao `fOp(x, y)`.
279	
280	**Primjer – Calculator.cs (s imenovanim metodama):**
281	
282	```csharp
283	public class Calculator
284	{
285	    public Func<int, int, int> GetRandomOperation()
286	    {
287	        var rand = new Random();
288	        switch (rand.Next() % 3)
289	        {
290	            case 0: return Summation;
291	            case 1: return Substraction;
292	            case 2: return Multiplication;
293	        }
294	        return null;
295	    }
296	
297	    public int Summation(int a, int b) => a + b;
298	    public int Substraction(int a, int b) => a - b;
299	    public int Multiplication(int a, int b) => a * b;
300	}
301	```
302	
303	**Primjer – Calculator.cs (s inline lambda izrazima):**
304	
305	```csharp
306	public class Calculator
307	{
308	    public Func<int, int, int> GetRandomOperation()
309	    {
310	        var rand = new Random();
311	        switch (rand.Next() % 3)
312	        {
313	            case 0:
314	                return (a, b) => { return a + b; };
315	            case 1:
316	                return ((a, b) => a - b);
317	            case 2:
318	                return (a, b) => a * b;
319	        }
320	        return null;
321	    }
322	}
323	```
324	
325	Moguće je i ovakav objekt proslijediti kao parametar nekoj drugoj metodi:
326	
327	```csharp
328	class Program
329	{
330	    static void PrintOperation(int x, int y, Func<int, int, int> op)
331	    {
332	        int rez = op(x, y);
333	        System.Console.WriteLine(rez);
334	    }
335	
336	    static void Main(string[] args)
337	    {
338	        var calc = new Calculator();
339	        Func<int,int,int> fOp = calc.GetRandomOperation();
340	        PrintOperation(10, 5, fOp);
341	        System.Console.ReadKey();
342	    }
343	}
344	```
345	
346	LINQ funkcije koriste upravo `Func<>`, `Action<>` i `Predicate<>` objekte za obradu kolekcija:
347	
348	- **Predicate<>** – isto što i `Func<>`, ali s povratnom vrijednošću `bool`
349	- **Action<>** – isto što i `Func<>`, ali bez povratne vrijednosti
350	
351	---
352	
353	# Async arhitektura
354	
355	Ključne naredbe **async** i **await** omogućavaju izuzetno lagano korištenje pozadinskih dretvi za izvršavanje procesa koji bi inače potrošili prijeko potrebne resurse. Postoje i performansni benefiti od korištenja async arhitekture u odnosu na sinkronu – najčešće u desktop/mobile okruženju gdje je korištenje UI dretve za dulje operacije nepovoljno.
356	
357	Osim u desktop/klijentskim aplikacijama, async postaje bitan u kontekstu velikog broja paralelnih zahtjeva na server, jer ako pozive na bazu podataka odrađuje pozadinska dretva, oslobađaju se resursi za odrađivanje „običnih" zahtjeva za to vrijeme.
358	
359	## Klasa Task
360	
361	U C# async arhitekturi, ključnu ulogu ima klasa `Task`, kao i ključna riječ `async`.
362	
363	```csharp
364	Task t1 = Task.Run(() =>
365	{
366	    Console.WriteLine($"Sleeping started");
367	    Thread.Sleep(1000);
368	    Console.WriteLine($"Sleeping completed");
369	});
370	Console.WriteLine($"Waiting on task..");
371	t1.Wait();
372	```
373	
374	Opis izvođenja:
375	
376	1. Kreiramo Task na način da mu definiramo što radi – to je primjer `Action` objekta iz prošlog poglavlja
377	    - Pozivom `Task.Run()` se taj task automatski pokreće
378	    - Spremamo ga u varijablu `t1`. U ovom trenutku task se već počeo izvršavati (vjerojatno)
379	2. Main program nastavlja daljnje izvođenje
380	3. Nakon toga zaustavljamo daljnje izvođenje dok god Task t1 ne završi: pozivom `t1.Wait()`
381	    - Alternativa `t1.Wait` bi bila statička metoda `Task.WaitAll(t1)` koja omogućava čekanje više taskova odjednom
382	
383	## Async metode
384	
385	Kada bi htjeli postići istu funkcionalnost pomoću async metode:
386	
387	```csharp
388	private static async Task DoSomeSleepingAsync()
389	{
390	    Console.WriteLine($"Sleeping started");
391	    await Task.Delay(1000);
392	    Console.WriteLine($"Sleeping completed");
393	}
394	
395	static void Main(string[] args)
396	{
397	    Task t1 = DoSomeSleepingAsync();
398	    Console.WriteLine($"Waiting on task..");
399	    t1.Wait();
400	    ...
401	}
402	```
403	
404	Razlika je u korištenju `await Task.Delay()` umjesto `Thread.Sleep()`. Analiza izvođenja:
405	
406	1. U Main metodi se kreira Task t1 pozivom async metode
407	    - Metoda `DoSomeSleepingAsync` ima definiranu povratnu vrijednost tipa `Task`, ali ne vraća nikakav rezultat (nema `return` naredbe). To odrađuje ključna riječ `async`.
408	2. Izvršava se prva linija unutar async metode
409	3. Nakon toga se dolazi do **await** naredbe. U tom trenutku se interno kreira nova dretva (thread) i izvršava spavanje od 1000ms. Za to vrijeme, u Main funkciji se nastavlja izvođenje paralelno
410	4. Kod poziva `t1.Wait()` se čeka da gornji Task završi
411	
412	Generalno, često će async metode imati sufiks „Async" tako da ih je vrlo lako prepoznati. Razne popularne biblioteke će često imati istu metodu napisanu u sinkronom kontekstu i async kontekstu.

Perfect! I now have the key architectural content. Let me compile the summary based on the information extracted:

## Summary: Competitive Coding IDE Architecture & Domain Model

### Overall Architecture

**Stack**: Dockerized monorepo with separate containerized services:
- **Frontend**: React with Vite (dev), Nginx reverse proxy (prod) with TypeScript
- **Backend**: ASP.NET MVC (.NET 8) with file-scoped namespaces and primary constructors
- **Real-time**: SignalR hub for live code execution feedback
- **Execution**: Isolated code sandbox (Docker-in-Docker or gVisor) for C/C++ submissions
- **Data**: PostgreSQL for persistence, Redis for caching/sessions
- **Routing**: Nginx at reverse proxy routing `/app/*` to frontend, `/api/*` to backend

**Deployment Stages**:
- **Stage 1 (100 users)**: Single 4vCPU/8GB VPS (Hetzner CX41 ~16€/month) with 2 concurrent sandbox runners
- **Stage 2 (500 users)**: Split app node (4vCPU/8GB) + worker node (8vCPU/16GB) with 6 concurrent runners, managed PostgreSQL
- **Stage 3+ (5K+ users)**: Kubernetes cluster with auto-scaling, multiple worker pools, read replicas

**Key Scaling Insight**: C/C++ fast execution (~1s compile + ~2s run) means sandbox becomes the bottleneck—API/DB scale horizontally but sandbox runners need dedicated CPU/RAM for deterministic timing guarantees.

### Domain Model (LeetCode-style Competitive Platform)

**Four Entity Clusters**:

1. **Identity**: Users (with roles: admin/instructor/student), Organizations, OrgMembers
2. **Content**: Problems (title, difficulty, constraints), ProblemTemplates (per-language starter code), ProblemSets (course groupings), TestCases, Tags
3. **Evaluation**: Submissions, ExecutionResults, TestCaseResults, ExecutionLogs (streamed chunks for replay)
4. **Social**: LeaderboardEntries (gamified with tier: bronze/silver/gold/platinum)

**Test Case Model**: Function-based evaluation—`input_args` (JSON array) deserialized, function called with args, return value compared against `expected_output` (JSON). `is_sample` flag controls visible vs hidden test cases.

**Three-Layer Execution Tracking**:
- `submissions`: status, score, perf summary
- `execution_results`: raw sandbox output (stdout/stderr/exit code, wall/CPU time, peak memory)
- `execution_logs`: individual streamed chunks in sequence order (powers SignalR real-time replay)

**Constraint Model**: Lives at three levels—
- Global platform defaults
- Per-problem limits (base_time_limit_ms, base_memory_limit_kb, sandbox_tier)
- Per-execution actuals (measured by sandbox, compared for pass/fail)

**Language-Specific Behavior**: Currently C/C++ only (no language multipliers needed—simplified single sandbox_tier). Expandable via language_configs if adding Python/Java later.

**Leaderboard**: Materialized denormalized view—updated on submission pass, tracks problems_solved, total_submissions, score, streaks, tier assignment, time/memory percentiles.

**Problem Sets**: Optional org-owned groupings for courses/playlists, with ordering and required-pass constraints for sequential unlock.

### Key C# Patterns

- **Thin Controllers** with Options pattern for configuration
- **EF Core skip navigation** for implicit many-to-many (problems ↔ tags)
- **Async patterns** throughout for I/O-heavy sandbox communication
- **LINQ queries** optimized with proper indexing (migrations include common query paths)
- **SignalR for real-time** streaming execution output (stdout/stderr chunks)

### Domain-Specific Adaptations

**Competitive Coding Focus**:
- Deterministic timeout/memory enforcement (no overcommit on sandbox workers)
- Per-submission execution history & replay capability
- Test case weighting and custom comparison modes (exact/float tolerance/unordered)
- Performance ranking (time/memory percentiles alongside score)
- Kill signals for OOM/timeout (tracked in execution_results)

**Architectural Decision**: C/C++ single-language focus (for now) keeps sandbox overhead minimal and execution ~3s per submission, enabling ~120 submissions/minute per 8-core worker node. This drives the infrastructure scaling model.

---

**File Reference**: `/Users/fbeleta/Documents/Web-ide/Web_ide/initialChat.json` contains the full design conversation including ERD diagrams, scaling calculations, and technology decision rationales.

# Lab-1: C# Object Model for Competitive Coding IDE

## Context
Lab-1 requires building a C# object model themed around your own project — a competitive coding web IDE (LeetCode-style). The model must demonstrate: 7+ classes (4 complex with 5+ properties), custom enums, DateTime properties, 1-N and N-N relationships, data population, LINQ queries, and async/await patterns. This is a standalone console app, NOT the full web IDE.

**Deadline**: April 2, 2026 at 18:00 (today)

---

## Project Structure

```
Web_ide/
├── lab-1/                        ← AI agent usage logs go here (per assignment)
├── WebIde.sln
├── WebIde.Model/
│   ├── WebIde.Model.csproj
│   ├── Enums/
│   │   ├── DifficultyLevel.cs    (Easy, Medium, Hard)
│   │   ├── SubmissionStatus.cs   (Pending, Running, Accepted, WrongAnswer, TimeLimitExceeded, MemoryLimitExceeded, CompileError)
│   │   └── UserRole.cs           (Admin, Instructor, Student)
│   ├── User.cs                   (complex: 5+ props)
│   ├── Organization.cs
│   ├── Problem.cs                (complex: 5+ props)
│   ├── ProblemSet.cs             (complex: 5+ props)
│   ├── Tag.cs
│   ├── TestCase.cs               (complex: 5+ props)
│   ├── Submission.cs             (complex: 5+ props, has DateTime)
│   └── ExecutionResult.cs
└── WebIde.Console/
    ├── WebIde.Console.csproj
    └── Program.cs
```

---

## Domain Model

### Enums
- `DifficultyLevel`: Easy, Medium, Hard
- `SubmissionStatus`: Pending, Running, Accepted, WrongAnswer, TimeLimitExceeded, MemoryLimitExceeded, CompileError
- `UserRole`: Admin, Instructor, Student

### Classes (8 total, 4 complex)

#### User (complex)
- `int Id`, `string Username`, `string Email`, `UserRole Role`
- `DateTime RegisteredAt`, `string DisplayName`
- `List<Submission> Submissions` (1-N: user has many submissions)
- `List<Organization> Organizations` (N-N via OrgMembership)

#### Organization
- `int Id`, `string Name`, `string Description`
- `List<User> Members` (N-N with User)
- `List<ProblemSet> ProblemSets` (1-N)

#### Problem (complex)
- `int Id`, `string Title`, `string Description`, `DifficultyLevel Difficulty`
- `int TimeLimitMs`, `int MemoryLimitKb`, `DateTime CreatedAt`, `string AuthorUsername`
- `List<TestCase> TestCases` (1-N)
- `List<Tag> Tags` (N-N)
- `List<Submission> Submissions` (1-N)

#### Tag
- `int Id`, `string Name`
- `List<Problem> Problems` (N-N with Problem)

#### TestCase (complex)
- `int Id`, `string InputArgs`, `string ExpectedOutput`
- `bool IsSample`, `int OrderIndex`, `int Points`
- Navigation: `Problem Problem`

#### Submission (complex)
- `int Id`, `string SourceCode`, `string Language`
- `SubmissionStatus Status`, `DateTime SubmittedAt`, `int Score`
- `int WallTimeMs`, `int PeakMemoryKb`
- Navigation: `User User`, `Problem Problem`, `ExecutionResult ExecutionResult`

#### ExecutionResult
- `int Id`, `string Stdout`, `string Stderr`
- `int ExitCode`, `bool TimedOut`, `bool MemoryExceeded`

#### ProblemSet (complex)
- `int Id`, `string Title`, `string Description`
- `DateTime CreatedAt`, `bool IsPublic`, `int OrderIndex`
- `List<Problem> Problems` (N-N with Problem)
- Navigation: `Organization Organization`

---

## Data Population (Program.cs)

Create 3 Problems (analogous to "3 tournaments with 3+ teams"), each with 3+ TestCases:
1. **"Two Sum"** (Easy) — 3 test cases
2. **"Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters"** (Medium) — 3 test cases  
3. **"Median of Two Sorted Arrays"** (Hard) — 4 test cases

Also create:
- 3 Users with different roles
- 1 Organization with ProblemSet grouping the 3 problems
- Tags: "Arrays", "Sliding Window", "Binary Search", "Hash Map"
- Several Submissions with ExecutionResults for each user/problem combo

---

## LINQ Queries

Meaningful queries relevant to a coding platform:
1. Filter problems by difficulty (where + toList)
2. Find all accepted submissions for a user (where + orderBy date desc)
3. Get problems tagged "Arrays" (where with subquery on Tags)
4. Top 3 scorers on a problem (orderByDescending score + take 3)
5. Count submissions per problem (count grouped by problem)
6. Find problems with no accepted submissions (where + subquery + !Any())
7. Get sample test cases for a problem (where IsSample + single/first)

---

## Async Demo

Show async/await concepts in Program.cs:
- `SimulateCodeExecutionAsync(Submission s)` — async method using `Task.Delay` (mimics sandbox compile+run)
- Run 3 submissions in parallel with `Task.WhenAll()`
- Show difference between `Task.Wait()` (blocking) and `await` (non-blocking)

---

## Critical Files to Create
1. `WebIde.sln` — solution file
2. `WebIde.Model/WebIde.Model.csproj` — class library (.NET 8)
3. `WebIde.Console/WebIde.Console.csproj` — console app (.NET 8, references Model)
4. All model classes and enums (11 files)
5. `WebIde.Console/Program.cs` — main program with data + LINQ + async

---

## Step-by-Step Execution Order
1. `dotnet new sln -n WebIde`
2. `dotnet new classlib -n WebIde.Model -f net8.0`
3. `dotnet new console -n WebIde.Console -f net8.0`
4. `dotnet sln add` both projects
5. `dotnet add WebIde.Console reference WebIde.Model`

content.text:
Good question. In the architecture from `initialChat.json`, `IsSample` controls whether a test case is **visible to the user** or hidden:

- `IsSample = true` → the test case is shown to the user in the problem description (so they can see example input/output and understand the problem)
- `IsSample = false` → hidden test case, only used during judging (prevents users from hardcoding outputs)

This maps directly to LeetCode's "Example 1, Example 2..." (visible) vs. the secret test cases used to actually grade submissions.

content.text:
Exactly right. With nullable reference types enabled (default in .NET 6+), the compiler tracks that `string` means "never null" — but your properties have no constructor initializing them, so they could be `null` at runtime. Two proper fixes:

- `required` on string properties that **must** be set at construction → compiler error if you forget them in an object initializer
- `?` on navigation properties (e.g. `Problem?`) → explicitly says "this reference may be null"

Clean build, 0 warnings. Everything works as expected:

- **LINQ queries** — all 7 fire correctly (filtering by difficulty, subquery on tags, ordering by score/time, count per problem, etc.)
- **Async** — you can see the 3 submissions interleaving (`Compiling` for all 3 before any `Running`) — that's `Task.WhenAll` in action vs. sequential execution
- **`required`** — the compiler now enforces that any code creating these objects must set the marked properties, so null safety is enforced at compile time

One notable design decision: `Submission.ExecutionResult` is `ExecutionResult?` (nullable) — that's intentional since a submission starts without a result (status = `Pending`) and only gets one after execution completes.

# WebIde

A competitive coding web IDE and evaluator — think LeetCode, but self-hosted and built from scratch as a learning project. Users submit code, it runs in an isolated sandbox, and results are streamed back in real time.

## Status

Early development. The domain model layer (Lab-1) is complete. The web application (ASP.NET MVC backend + React/TypeScript frontend) is next.

## Planned Stack

| Layer | Technology |
|---|---|
| Frontend | React + TypeScript (Vite dev, Nginx prod) |
| Backend | ASP.NET MVC (.NET 10) |
| Real-time | SignalR |
| Database | PostgreSQL |
| Cache / Sessions | Redis |
| Code Execution | Isolated sandbox (Docker) |
| Reverse Proxy | Nginx |

## Project Structure

```
WebIde.Model/       — Domain model: classes, enums, relationships
WebIde.Console/     — Lab-1 console app: data population, LINQ queries, async demo
docs/               — Architecture and design documentation
lab-1/              — AI agent usage logs (course requirement)
```

## Running the Console App (Lab-1)

Requires [.NET 10 SDK](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download).

```bash
dotnet run --project WebIde.Console
```

## Documentation

- [Architecture](docs/architecture.md) — system design, layers, scaling plan
- [Domain Model](docs/domain-model.md) — all classes, enums, and their relationships

input.content:
# Architecture

## Overview

WebIde is a self-hosted competitive coding platform. Users write code in a browser-based editor, submit it, and get results streamed back in real time. The system separates concerns into distinct layers: presentation, business logic, and code execution.

## Current State

The domain model layer (`WebIde.Model`) is implemented as a .NET class library. A console app (`WebIde.Console`) exercises the model with LINQ queries and async patterns. No web layer exists yet.

## Target Architecture

```
Browser
  └── React/TypeScript SPA
        │
        │ HTTP /api/*
        ▼
  ASP.NET MVC (.NET 10)          ← controllers, services, EF Core
        │
        ├── PostgreSQL            ← persistent storage
        ├── Redis                 ← sessions, caching
        └── Sandbox Worker        ← isolated code execution (Docker)
              │
              │ SignalR
              ▼
        Browser (real-time execution output stream)
```

Nginx sits in front of everything as a reverse proxy:
- `/app/*` → React frontend (Nginx static in prod, Vite dev server in dev)
- `/api/*` → ASP.NET MVC backend

## Layers

### WebIde.Model
Pure C# class library. No dependencies on any framework. Contains:
- Domain entity classes (Problem, Submission, User, etc.)
- Enums (DifficultyLevel, SubmissionStatus, UserRole)

This library is referenced by any layer that needs to work with the domain — backend API, tests, etc.

### Backend (planned)
ASP.NET MVC (.NET 10). Responsibilities:
- REST API for the frontend
- Business logic (submission scoring, leaderboard updates)
- EF Core ORM talking to PostgreSQL
- SignalR hub for streaming execution output

### Frontend (planned)
React + TypeScript, bundled with Vite. Responsibilities:
- Code editor (Monaco Editor)
- Problem browser, submission history, leaderboard
- SignalR client for real-time output

### Sandbox (planned)
Isolated execution environment for untrusted code. Each submission gets:
- A container with strict CPU, memory, and time limits
- No network access
- Results (stdout, stderr, exit code, wall time, peak memory) written back to the backend

## Scaling Plan

| Stage | Users | Infrastructure |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ~100 | Single VPS (4 vCPU / 8 GB), 2 sandbox runners |
| 2 | ~500 | App node + dedicated worker node, managed PostgreSQL |
| 3+ | 5 000+ | Kubernetes, auto-scaling worker pools, read replicas |

The bottleneck at scale is the sandbox, not the API or database. C/C++ submissions take ~1s compile + ~2s run, so sandbox runners need dedicated CPU for deterministic timing.

input.content:
# Domain Model

All classes live in `WebIde.Model`. The model is a pure class library with no framework dependencies — it can be referenced by any layer.

## Enums

### `DifficultyLevel`
Classifies problem difficulty.
```
Easy | Medium | Hard
```

### `SubmissionStatus`
Tracks the lifecycle of a code submission.
```
Pending → Running → Accepted
                  → WrongAnswer
                  → TimeLimitExceeded
                  → MemoryLimitExceeded
                  → CompileError
```

### `UserRole`
Controls what a user can do on the platform.
```
Admin | Instructor | Student
```

---

## Classes

### `User`
A registered platform user.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Username | string | required |
| Email | string | required |
| DisplayName | string | required |
| Role | UserRole | |
| RegisteredAt | DateTime | |
| Submissions | List\<Submission\> | 1-N: user has many submissions |
| Organizations | List\<Organization\> | N-N: user belongs to many orgs |

---

### `Organization`
A group of users — represents a course, team, or institution.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Name | string | required |
| Description | string | required |
| Members | List\<User\> | N-N with User |
| ProblemSets | List\<ProblemSet\> | 1-N: org owns many problem sets |

---

### `Problem`
A coding problem with a description and constraints.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Title | string | required |
| Description | string | required |
| Difficulty | DifficultyLevel | |
| TimeLimitMs | int | execution time cap per submission |
| MemoryLimitKb | int | memory cap per submission |
| CreatedAt | DateTime | |
| AuthorUsername | string | required |
| TestCases | List\<TestCase\> | 1-N: problem has many test cases |
| Tags | List\<Tag\> | N-N: problem has many tags |
| Submissions | List\<Submission\> | 1-N: problem has many submissions |

---

### `Tag`
A label for categorizing problems (e.g. "Arrays", "Binary Search").

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Name | string | required |
| Problems | List\<Problem\> | N-N with Problem |

---

### `TestCase`
A single input/output pair used to judge a submission.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| InputArgs | string | required — serialized input (e.g. JSON array) |
| ExpectedOutput | string | required |
| IsSample | bool | true = shown to user; false = hidden judge-only |
| OrderIndex | int | display/execution order |
| Points | int | partial scoring weight |
| Problem | Problem | required — owning problem |

---

### `Submission`
One attempt by a user to solve a problem.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| SourceCode | string | required |
| Language | string | required — e.g. "cpp", "c" |
| Status | SubmissionStatus | |
| SubmittedAt | DateTime | |
| Score | int | 0–100, based on passing test cases |
| WallTimeMs | int | measured execution time |
| PeakMemoryKb | int | measured peak memory |
| User | User | required |
| Problem | Problem | required |
| ExecutionResult | ExecutionResult? | null while status is Pending/Running |

---

### `ExecutionResult`
Raw output from the sandbox for a submission.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Stdout | string | required |
| Stderr | string | required |
| ExitCode | int | 0 = clean exit |
| TimedOut | bool | true if wall time exceeded limit |
| MemoryExceeded | bool | true if peak memory exceeded limit |

---

### `ProblemSet`
An ordered collection of problems — used for courses or curated playlists.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Title | string | required |
| Description | string | required |
| CreatedAt | DateTime | |
| IsPublic | bool | controls visibility to non-members |
| OrderIndex | int | ordering within the organization |
| Organization | Organization | required — owning org |
| Problems | List\<Problem\> | N-N with Problem |

---

## Relationships

```
Organization ──< ProblemSet >──< Problem >──< Tag
                                    │
                                    └──< TestCase
                                    └──< Submission >── ExecutionResult
                                              │
User ──────────────────────────────────────── ┘
 └──< Organization (N-N)
```

Key relationships:
- **1-N**: Problem → TestCases, Problem → Submissions, User → Submissions, Organization → ProblemSets
- **N-N**: Problem ↔ Tag, Problem ↔ ProblemSet, User ↔ Organization
- `ExecutionResult` is nullable on `Submission` — it only exists after the sandbox has finished running

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Name | string | required |
| Problems | List\<Problem\> | N-N with Problem |

---

### `TestCase`
A single input/output pair used to judge a submission.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| InputArgs | string | required — serialized input (e.g. JSON array) |
| ExpectedOutput | string | required |
| IsSample | bool | true = shown to user; false = hidden judge-only |
| OrderIndex | int | display/execution order |
| Points | int | partial scoring weight |
| Problem | Problem | required — owning problem |

---

### `Submission`
One attempt by a user to solve a problem.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| SourceCode | string | required |
| Language | string | required — e.g. "cpp", "c" |
| Status | SubmissionStatus | |
| SubmittedAt | DateTime | |
| Score | int | 0–100, based on passing test cases |
| WallTimeMs | int | measured execution time |
| PeakMemoryKb | int | measured peak memory |
| User | User | required |
| Problem | Problem | required |
| ExecutionResult | ExecutionResult? | null while status is Pending/Running |

---

### `ExecutionResult`
Raw output from the sandbox for a submission.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Stdout | string | required |
| Stderr | string | required |
| ExitCode | int | 0 = clean exit |
| TimedOut | bool | true if wall time exceeded limit |
| MemoryExceeded | bool | true if peak memory exceeded limit |

---

### `ProblemSet`
An ordered collection of problems — used for courses or curated playlists.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Title | string | required |
| Description | string | required |
| CreatedAt | DateTime | |
| IsPublic | bool | controls visibility to non-members |
| OrderIndex | int | ordering within the organization |
| Organization | Organization | required — owning org |
| Problems | List\<Problem\> | N-N with Problem |

---

## Relationships

```
Organization ──< ProblemSet >──< Problem >──< Tag
                                    │
                                    └──< TestCase
                                    └──< Submission >── ExecutionResult
                                              │
User ──────────────────────────────────────── ┘
 └──< Organization (N-N)
```

Key relationships:
- **1-N**: Problem → TestCases, Problem → Submissions, User → Submissions, Organization → ProblemSets
- **N-N**: Problem ↔ Tag, Problem ↔ ProblemSet, User ↔ Organization
- `ExecutionResult` is nullable on `Submission` — it only exists after the sandbox has finished running

**`docs/architecture.md`** — system design diagram, layer responsibilities, scaling plan from the initial architecture conversation.

**`docs/domain-model.md`** — full reference for every class and enum: property tables with types/notes, the `IsSample` distinction, and a relationship diagram at the bottom.

The docs are intentionally written as living documents — sparse where things don't exist yet (backend, frontend, sandbox are marked "planned"), detailed where they do. As you build each layer you just fill in the relevant section.

message.content: id like to explore the codebase a bit to get more familiar with asp and its structure as well as this specific projects structure. why do we need the tag model? wouldnt this usually be done by .filter or somehting similar on the frontend or is this so that we have a standardized return type where we can directly query for what we need plus reduce the call size so the load is lighter?

content.text:
Good instinct — you're already thinking about where logic should live. Let me answer directly.

**Both of your reasons are correct, but the bigger reason is data integrity and query efficiency.**

If tags were just a `string[]` on `Problem` (like `["Arrays", "Hash Map"]`), you'd have these problems:

1. **No consistency** — "arrays", "Arrays", "array" are all different strings. Nothing stops typos or duplicates from creeping in.
2. **Filtering still has to happen somewhere** — if tags are raw strings, the frontend calls `/api/problems` and gets *all* problems back, then filters client-side. With 10,000 problems that's a massive payload for a filter that could have been one SQL `WHERE` clause.
3. **You can't query by tag efficiently** — with a proper `Tag` table and a join table, the database can use an index. With a string array you're doing a full table scan with a `LIKE` or `ANY()`.
4. **Tags become a first-class resource** — you can build a `/api/tags` endpoint to power a tag browser/autocomplete, show "342 problems tagged Arrays", etc. None of that is possible if tags are just strings embedded in problems.

So the flow ends up being:

```
Frontend requests: GET /api/problems?tag=Arrays&difficulty=Medium
                                       ↓
Backend runs:  problems.Where(p => p.Tags.Any(t => t.Name == "Arrays")
                               && p.Difficulty == DifficultyLevel.Medium)
                                       ↓
Returns only the matching subset — small, typed, consistent payload
```

The frontend never sees the 9,950 problems it didn't ask for.

---

**On ASP.NET MVC structure** — since you want to get familiar, here's how the model layer you just built connects upward when we add the web layer:

```
WebIde.Model          ← you built this (plain C# classes, no framework)
    ↑
WebIde.DAL (later)    ← EF Core: maps your classes to PostgreSQL tables
    ↑                    Tag becomes a "tags" table, Problem↔Tag becomes
    ↑                    a "problem_tags" join table automatically
WebIde.Services       ← business logic: "score this submission", "update leaderboard"
    ↑
WebIde.Web (ASP.NET)  ← controllers receive HTTP requests, call services,
                         return JSON responses to the React frontend
```

The reason Model is its own project with no dependencies is exactly so that every layer above can reference it without pulling in framework baggage. A controller, a service, and a test can all use `Problem` without knowing anything about each other.

The `Tag` class in the model is small now, but it's the anchor that makes the whole join relationship work properly once EF Core enters the picture — it maps directly to a `tags` table, and the `List<Tag> Tags` on `Problem` maps to a `problem_tags` join table.

message.content: so instead of handling it all frontend we delegate to the backend which makes sense, and then the tags table contains just something like id | tag and we join it with the problem table to get a full picture?

content.text:
Exactly. The `tags` table is just:

```
tags
─────────────
id  | name
────┼─────────
1   | Arrays
2   | Hash Map
3   | Sliding Window
4   | Binary Search
```

And because it's N-N (a problem can have many tags, a tag can belong to many problems), you can't put a foreign key directly on either table. So EF Core generates a third **join table** automatically:

```
problem_tags
──────────────────
problem_id | tag_id
───────────┼───────
1          | 1      ← Two Sum → Arrays
1          | 2      ← Two Sum → Hash Map
2          | 3      ← Longest Substring → Sliding Window
2          | 2      ← Longest Substring → Hash Map
3          | 4      ← Median → Binary Search
```

Then when you query `problems WHERE tag = "Arrays"`, the database does:

```sql
SELECT p.*
FROM problems p
JOIN problem_tags pt ON pt.problem_id = p.id
JOIN tags t          ON t.id = pt.tag_id
WHERE t.name = 'Arrays'
```

Three small indexed lookups instead of scanning every problem row. The `List<Tag> Tags` property on your `Problem` class is what tells EF Core to generate that join table — it reads the relationship from both sides (`Problem` has `List<Tag>`, `Tag` has `List<Problem>`) and knows it needs the middle table. You never write `problem_tags` yourself.

message.content: alirght that makes a lot of sense, and the models, they arent just patterns like .schema but full classes in which we can implement methods lateR?
the purpose of LINQ then is to not write plain SQL but to have another layer, something like gql for graph databases?

content.text:
Close, but the analogy is slightly different. LINQ isn't a separate layer over the database — it's a **query language built into C#** that works on anything enumerable: in-memory lists, database tables, XML, whatever.

The better mental model is this:

```
Your C# code
    │
    │  LINQ  (.Where, .OrderBy, .Select ...)
    ▓
    │
    ├── List<Problem>          → executes in memory, plain C# logic
    │
    └── EF Core DbSet<Problem> → translates to SQL, executes in the database
```

Same syntax, two completely different execution paths depending on what you're querying. Right now in `Program.cs` all your LINQ runs **in memory** because `problems` is a `List<T>`. Once EF Core is in the picture, the exact same query runs **as SQL in PostgreSQL**:

```csharp
// Today — runs in memory, C# evaluates it
var easy = problems.Where(p => p.Difficulty == DifficultyLevel.Easy).ToList();

// Later with EF Core — LINQ translates to SQL, database evaluates it
var easy = await dbContext.Problems
    .Where(p => p.Difficulty == DifficultyLevel.Easy)
    .ToListAsync();
```

EF Core's job is translating that second one into:

```sql
SELECT * FROM problems WHERE difficulty = 0
```

So the comparison to GraphQL isn't quite right — GraphQL is a **protocol** between client and server. LINQ is more like having SQL syntax available natively in your language, with the bonus that it works identically whether you're filtering a database table or a plain list in memory. That's what makes it powerful — you write the same query logic regardless of where the data lives.

message.content: ahhh so its an adaptation of SQL for C# native database querying?

content.text:
Pretty much, yes. Though it's slightly broader than that — it was designed as a **universal query syntax for C#**, not specifically for databases. But in practice, querying databases through EF Core is its most common and most powerful use.

The key insight is the translation chain:

```
LINQ expression
      ↓
EF Core reads it as an "expression tree" (a description of what you want)
      ↓
Translates it to SQL dialect of your database (PostgreSQL, SQL Server, SQLite...)
      ↓
Database executes it, returns rows
      ↓
EF Core maps rows back to your C# objects (Problem, User, etc.)
```

That last step — rows back to objects — is actually the other half of EF Core's job, called **object-relational mapping (ORM)**. LINQ handles the querying side, ORM handles the mapping side. EF Core does both.

The practical benefit for this project is that you never write raw SQL. You write C# against your model classes, and EF Core figures out the SQL. If you switch from PostgreSQL to a different database later, you swap the EF Core provider and your LINQ queries stay untouched.

The one thing worth knowing early: LINQ over EF Core has limits. Very complex queries sometimes don't translate cleanly, and EF Core will either throw an error or silently pull everything into memory and filter there — which kills performance. When that happens you drop down to raw SQL through EF Core's `FromSqlRaw()`. But for the vast majority of queries like what you have now, LINQ translates perfectly.

A competitive coding web IDE and evaluator — think LeetCode, but self-hosted and built from scratch as a learning project. Users submit code, it runs in an isolated sandbox, and results are streamed back in real time.

## Status

Early development. The domain model layer (Lab-1) is complete. The web application (ASP.NET MVC backend + React/TypeScript frontend) is next.

## Planned Stack

| Layer | Technology |
|---|---|
| Frontend | React + TypeScript (Vite dev, Nginx prod) |
| Backend | ASP.NET MVC (.NET 10) |
| Real-time | SignalR |
| Database | PostgreSQL |
| Cache / Sessions | Redis |
| Code Execution | Isolated sandbox (Docker) |
| Reverse Proxy | Nginx |

## Project Structure

```
WebIde.Model/       — Domain model: classes, enums, relationships
WebIde.Console/     — Lab-1 console app: data population, LINQ queries, async demo
docs/               — Architecture and design documentation
lab-1/              — AI agent usage logs (course requirement)
```

## Running the Console App (Lab-1)

Requires [.NET 10 SDK](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download).

dotnet run --project WebIde.Console
```

## Documentation

- [Architecture](docs/architecture.md) — system design, layers, scaling plan
- [Domain Model](docs/domain-model.md) — all classes, enums, and their relationships

# Architecture

## Overview

WebIde is a self-hosted competitive coding platform. Users write code in a browser-based editor, submit it, and get results streamed back in real time. The system separates concerns into distinct layers: presentation, business logic, and code execution.

## Current State

The domain model layer (`WebIde.Model`) is implemented as a .NET class library. A console app (`WebIde.Console`) exercises the model with LINQ queries and async patterns. No web layer exists yet.

## Target Architecture

```
Browser
  └── React/TypeScript SPA
        │
        │ HTTP /api/*
        ▼
  ASP.NET MVC (.NET 10)          ← controllers, services, EF Core
        │
        ├── PostgreSQL            ← persistent storage
        ├── Redis                 ← sessions, caching
        └── Sandbox Worker        ← isolated code execution (Docker)
              │
              │ SignalR
              ▼
        Browser (real-time execution output stream)
```

Nginx sits in front of everything as a reverse proxy:
- `/app/*` → React frontend (Nginx static in prod, Vite dev server in dev)
- `/api/*` → ASP.NET MVC backend

## Layers

### WebIde.Model
Pure C# class library. No dependencies on any framework. Contains:
- Domain entity classes (Problem, Submission, User, etc.)
- Enums (DifficultyLevel, SubmissionStatus, UserRole)

This library is referenced by any layer that needs to work with the domain — backend API, tests, etc.

### Backend (planned)
ASP.NET MVC (.NET 10). Responsibilities:
- REST API for the frontend
- Business logic (submission scoring, leaderboard updates)
- EF Core ORM talking to PostgreSQL
- SignalR hub for streaming execution output

### Frontend (planned)
React + TypeScript, bundled with Vite. Responsibilities:
- Code editor (Monaco Editor)
- Problem browser, submission history, leaderboard
- SignalR client for real-time output

### Sandbox (planned)
Isolated execution environment for untrusted code. Each submission gets:
- A container with strict CPU, memory, and time limits
- No network access
- Results (stdout, stderr, exit code, wall time, peak memory) written back to the backend

## Scaling Plan

| Stage | Users | Infrastructure |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ~100 | Single VPS (4 vCPU / 8 GB), 2 sandbox runners |
| 2 | ~500 | App node + dedicated worker node, managed PostgreSQL |
| 3+ | 5 000+ | Kubernetes, auto-scaling worker pools, read replicas |

The bottleneck at scale is the sandbox, not the API or database. C/C++ submissions take ~1s compile + ~2s run, so sandbox runners need dedicated CPU for deterministic timing.

# Domain Model

All classes live in `WebIde.Model`. The model is a pure class library with no framework dependencies — it can be referenced by any layer.

## Enums

### `DifficultyLevel`
Classifies problem difficulty.
```
Easy | Medium | Hard
```

### `SubmissionStatus`
Tracks the lifecycle of a code submission.
```
Pending → Running → Accepted
                  → WrongAnswer
                  → TimeLimitExceeded
                  → MemoryLimitExceeded
                  → CompileError
```

### `UserRole`
Controls what a user can do on the platform.
```
Admin | Instructor | Student
```

---

## Classes

### `User`
A registered platform user.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Username | string | required |
| Email | string | required |
| DisplayName | string | required |
| Role | UserRole | |
| RegisteredAt | DateTime | |
| Submissions | List\<Submission\> | 1-N: user has many submissions |
| Organizations | List\<Organization\> | N-N: user belongs to many orgs |

---

### `Organization`
A group of users — represents a course, team, or institution.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Name | string | required |
| Description | string | required |
| Members | List\<User\> | N-N with User |
| ProblemSets | List\<ProblemSet\> | 1-N: org owns many problem sets |

---

### `Problem`
A coding problem with a description and constraints.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Title | string | required |
| Description | string | required |
| Difficulty | DifficultyLevel | |
| TimeLimitMs | int | execution time cap per submission |
| MemoryLimitKb | int | memory cap per submission |
| CreatedAt | DateTime | |
| AuthorUsername | string | required |
| TestCases | List\<TestCase\> | 1-N: problem has many test cases |
| Tags | List\<Tag\> | N-N: problem has many tags |
| Submissions | List\<Submission\> | 1-N: problem has many submissions |

---

### `Tag`
A label for categorizing problems (e.g. "Arrays", "Binary Search").

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Name | string | required |
| Problems | List\<Problem\> | N-N with Problem |

---

### `TestCase`
A single input/output pair used to judge a submission.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| InputArgs | string | required — serialized input (e.g. JSON array) |
| ExpectedOutput | string | required |
| IsSample | bool | true = shown to user; false = hidden judge-only |
| OrderIndex | int | display/execution order |
| Points | int | partial scoring weight |
| Problem | Problem | required — owning problem |

---

### `Submission`
One attempt by a user to solve a problem.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| SourceCode | string | required |
| Language | string | required — e.g. "cpp", "c" |
| Status | SubmissionStatus | |
| SubmittedAt | DateTime | |
| Score | int | 0–100, based on passing test cases |
| WallTimeMs | int | measured execution time |
| PeakMemoryKb | int | measured peak memory |
| User | User | required |
| Problem | Problem | required |
| ExecutionResult | ExecutionResult? | null while status is Pending/Running |

---

### `ExecutionResult`
Raw output from the sandbox for a submission.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Stdout | string | required |
| Stderr | string | required |
| ExitCode | int | 0 = clean exit |
| TimedOut | bool | true if wall time exceeded limit |
| MemoryExceeded | bool | true if peak memory exceeded limit |

---

### `ProblemSet`
An ordered collection of problems — used for courses or curated playlists.

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Id | int | |
| Title | string | required |
| Description | string | required |
| CreatedAt | DateTime | |
| IsPublic | bool | controls visibility to non-members |
| OrderIndex | int | ordering within the organization |
| Organization | Organization | required — owning org |
| Problems | List\<Problem\> | N-N with Problem |

---

## Relationships

```
Organization ──< ProblemSet >──< Problem >──< Tag
                                    │
                                    └──< TestCase
                                    └──< Submission >── ExecutionResult
                                              │
User ──────────────────────────────────────── ┘
 └──< Organization (N-N)
```

Key relationships:
- **1-N**: Problem → TestCases, Problem → Submissions, User → Submissions, Organization → ProblemSets
- **N-N**: Problem ↔ Tag, Problem ↔ ProblemSet, User ↔ Organization
- `ExecutionResult` is nullable on `Submission` — it only exists after the sandbox has finished running

content.text:
Yes, exactly. GitHub natively renders Mermaid code blocks — so instead of seeing the raw text, you'll see a fully rendered ER diagram with boxes for each entity, their fields listed inside, and labeled arrows between them showing the relationships.

It looks like this when rendered:

```
┌─────────────┐        ┌─────────────┐
│    User     │        │ Submission  │
│─────────────│──o{────│─────────────│
│ Id          │        │ Id          │
│ Username    │        │ SourceCode  │
│ ...         │        │ ...         │
└─────────────┘        └─────────────┘
```

Except rendered properly with GitHub's styling. The `||--o{` notation is what controls those arrow shapes — GitHub knows how to read Mermaid's ERD syntax and draws it visually.

Only caveat: it only renders on **github.com** in the browser. Raw file view, local editors (without a Mermaid plugin), and PDF exports won't render it. For the lab submission mermaid.live export as an image is the safer bet if you need it in a document.
Appreciate it! Ready whenever you want to open the PR or move on to the next lab.

content.text:
The transcripts are stored at:

```
~/.claude/projects/
```

Each project gets a subfolder named after its path (sanitized). For this project it'll be something like:

```
~/.claude/projects/-Users-fbeleta-Documents-Web-ide-Web_ide/
```

Inside you'll find `.jsonl` files — one per session. You don't need to `/exit` first, the transcript is written continuously as the conversation progresses so it's already there. Just find the most recent `.jsonl` file and copy it.

You can run this to find it quickly:

```bash
ls -lt ~/.claude/projects/-Users-fbeleta-Documents-Web-ide-Web_ide/
```
